sentences.text: My name is Trude Levi.
T-R-U-D-E L-E-V-I.
Thank you.
And your name at birth.
No-- yes.
It's Mosonyi.
M-O-S-O-N-Y-I.
Well, I was married before.
And my former name was they Deaka, D-E-A-K-A with an accent.
23rd of April 1924.
And I am 71, nearly 72.
The city was Szombathely.
S-Z-O-M-B-A-T-H-E-L-Y.
And this is on the Austrian border in Hungary.
Well, I-- I was born in Szombathely.
And my father was a medical man.
He was a gynecologist and a general practitioner.
He was a Hungarian.
My mother came from Vienna.
And she was a language teacher.
She taught English and German and also shorthand in these languages.
And she taught privately at home.
My-- we had a nice home.
Not a very big one, but the nice one with lots of books and chamber music and pictures and a very civilized background.
A very cultured background, let us say.
We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father used to take me-- I had to brother.
I have a brother.
And he was 5 and 1/2 years older than I am.
He still is, sorry.
And his name is Pierre Mosonyi.
He used to be called Peter when-- when he was in Hungary.
My-- I-- my father used to take me on Sunday mornings for a walk.
And I enjoyed that very much when I was small.
Sometimes I suppose with my brother.
But I don't remember.
That I don't remember.
What I do remember, I'm saying this mainly, because of one walk.
When I was five, my father said that he's taking me for a walk.
And when we started walking he said, we will first go to a place where he had-- had a birth that night.
And he was rather worried about the lady who the baby.
And therefore, he wanted to look in at them.
And I would have to stand in the-- stay in the courtyard while he went in.
He took me to a very poor quarter of the town.
And he-- and I stood in a courtyard where there was one narrow window, one narrow door, one narrow window, one narrow door in a circular way round.
And I saw occasionally a curtain moving and somebody looking out.
And then my father went in.
And I was standing in the middle of the courtyard dressed in my Sunday best.
Because in Hungary you dressed up for Sunday, not like in England where you dress down for Sunday.
And I had a nice red coat on, I remember.
And then one boy came out from one door, and then another child came out from another door.
And some more children came out.
And suddenly, they started calling me dirty Jewish pig and started throwing stones at me.
And by the time-- time my father a came out, I was bleeding.
I was crying and bleeding.
And I asked my father what-- I said, well, I don't understand.
I am not dirty.
I had a bath.
And I am not a pig, because that's an animal.
And I'm not an animal.
What does it mean Jewish?
Now, I don't quite remember what my father said.
Something about it's a-- a people, I mean a-- a-- a group of people who have got a different religion.
But since we were not religious, it didn't matter very much.
But it was a group of people, a different religion.
And sometimes people who were a bit different were not liked or were-- were called names, which wasn't very nice.
And so I remember at that-- I told him all right, well, then from now on, I'm going to be Jewish.
And-- and from that moment on, my identity was Jewish.
I never felt myself Hungarian.
Though, I lived in Hungary until the age of 20.
But I never felt myself Hungarian.
And so I wasn't religious.
But I still was Jewish.
I was involved in various Jewish youth organizations.
I-- well, I became a Zionist at the age of nine and was in, I don't know, I suppose it was [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a rather odd group of Zionist youth.
Because we weren't very many.
In Hungary, there were very few Zionists.
My mother was a treasurer of the [NON-ENGLISH] and of the Zionist Women's Federation in town.
But I don't think they had very many members.
Because in Hungary, I know that my father refused to be a Zionist.
But I was.
And I was-- I became [NON-ENGLISH].
Because very, very early, I had a very social-- a very strong social conscience.
My father was a very committed socialist who wrote in newspapers.
He was pretty left-wing.
And, I mean, communism wasn't permitted in-- in Hungary.
So he wasn't a communist, but he was-- he couldn't be a communist.
But he was a very left-wing socialist.
And he wrote lots of articles.
Altogether, my father was-- an exceptionally brilliant man.
He was one of the first people to write about the psychology of music.
He played the violin and viola.
His article which appeared in the which was a magazine by Freud.
And there he had a letter of Freud.
My brother had the letter-- which he found after the-- after the war.
And he handed it in to-- to Vienna to the Freud Museum-- is where Freud wrote that he doesn't know much about music.
But he did print his essay on the psychology of music.
And this essay is up to-- up there today being quoted by anyone who writes about-- deals with the psychology of music.
I mean, there are an American publication which came out a couple of years ago, a French publication which came out a few years ago.
And again and again and again, his name still crops up.
He played the violin and the viola.
Well, my mother played the piano.
But I think that she was considered not very good.
My-- my brother was extremely gifted.
He sang, supposedly sang Haydn symphonies before he even spoke.
And he was altogether very bright.
And he became a professional pianist.
And-- and I was-- I was singing.
I could sing from any-- any music that was put in front of me from the age of nine.
And through my life, I used my knowledge.
I had a pretty decent voice.
Unfortunately, now it's practically gone, which is very sad.
But-- I had-- I-- I-- later on I sang in choirs and in important choirs, like the Philharmonia Choir here in its best days, in its top days.
Or-- and whenever I was hungry, then I sang in synagogue choirs or radio choirs or wherever they paid me a bad fee.
But I needed the money.
I sang.
But coming back to it, as I said my, father played.
So I-- I learned to play the cello.
As a youngster, I hated it.
But eventually, I quite liked it.
But I didn't-- never had proper tuition.
Because in town, there was no teacher.
And also in town, girls in Hungary didn't play the cello.
That wasn't an instrument that women played.
And when I went through town with my cello, all the kids were running after me calling me names.
And I hated every minute of it.
But eventually, I learned to play a little bit.
Later on, I mean, I still have got a cello standing here.
I haven't touched it for the last 25 years, but it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but because later on when I wanted to play the cello, I either had the money to take lessons, but no time or no cello.
Or, I had no cello.
I never had a cello and the money and the time at the same time.
I mean, for quite a long time after the war I didn't have an instrument anyway.
At-- at the age of 13-- well, as I said, my mother was from Vienna.
My grandmother lived in Vienna.
And my favorite aunt lived in Vienna.
And so I spent quite a lot of holidays in Vienna.
I don't-- I-- neither of my grandfathers was alive by the time I was born.
So I never knew them.
But that my grandmother on my father's side lived in a small village.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this village-- sorry.
[DRINKS] And-- and her-- well, my brother's sister, her daughter, was a piano teacher.
And her other son also played the violin, so that there was music already in the family.
And through my whole life, music played a rather important part.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- sorry, I--
I went to-- yes, I started my primary school in-- in my hometown.
Everybody, all Jewish children, went into the Jewish school.
Now, we had two Jewish congregations.
There was an Orthodox.
And there was the Neolog.
The Neolog is something in between United Synagogue and Reform synagogue.
Everything was done in Hebrew.
Religious education-- religion was compulsory in Hungary.
You couldn't be an atheist.
And you had to belong to some religion.
And a religious tax was deducted from everybody's salary, which went then to-- either to the synagogue-- but it was not one particular synagogue.
But-- I mean, yes, we belonged to the Neolog synagogue officially.
My parents never went to the synagogue.
But were had-- we were obliged to go to religion lessons.
And we had to-- and-- and I had very big difficulties with the religion teacher.
Because my brother was rather naughty, and also because I resented my parents not going to synagogue.
It was a provincial town.
And this teacher, who was at the time 60 years old and-- and not very patient used to beat me constantly.
And-- and I just couldn't learn with him at all.
So I failed my-- I-- I couldn't learn to read Hebrew, which was rather odd.
Because later on, I became a Hebrew teacher.
And I-- I-- couldn't-- so I-- I did-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I failed.
And I had two swat the whole summer to pass my exam in September.
Otherwise, I would have been kept down.
Because religious education was a compulsory subject.
And if you didn't pass it, then you had to repeat the whole year in all the subjects again.
Luckily, I passed.
And I didn't have to do that.

But this teacher treated me so badly that eventually, my father-- after the third year, my father made a petition to try and get him-- that he should be pensioned off and that he wasn't suitable to deal with small children.
And eventually, the other people who said they would sign, they didn't.
They opted out.
And so my father took me out and put me into-- for the fourth year, put me into a state school.
Because I was so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- because he so maltreated me, I just couldn't learn at all.
And therefore, if I had bad marks, since we had numerous classes in the-- in the higher schools, I couldn't have got into a-- a proper higher school if with my marks, with my low marks.
And therefore, he put me into another school, a state school where among 600 children I was only Jewish child.
And it was a rather tough year to go through.
But actually, it wasn't that bad.
And I got extremely good marks in that school.
I did-- I was one of the best in the class.
And that helped me then to get into a sort of grammar school, a girls' lyceum, which was sort of girls grammar school.
And I was there for five years.
When I was 13, we were-- no, just a minute.
That was-- anyway, 1938 the Germans walked into Austria.
And we-- the town-- we lived on the Austrian border.
And from the-- at the-- because we were in the Austrian border and we had family in Vienna, they brought over-- my father had quite a few clients, patients, in-- over the border.
And the-- the town was such that there was a mountain in between Austria and Hungary.
And we used to go into the mountains nearly every Sunday, or very, very often.
We went into the mountains and went over the border regularly.
So the border guards knew us quite well.
And so all my grandmother and-- and my aunt all sent down jewels and all kinds of belongings to patients in-- on the Australian side.
And we went over every Sunday and brought over.
So we were sitting in the-- in the forest on a Persian carpet, eating with silver-- [LAUGHTER] in silver dishes, which was rather funny.
When the-- I mean, so that we were using those for the border guards who let us through, who-- And we brought over everything, I mean, lots of things, incredible amount of things from--
That was-- there was no difficulty at all for us to bring it over.
But, of course, everything was lost anyway later on. in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so when I was 15-- this was in '38.

In-- when I was the 15, my Austrian family, who in the meantime emigrated, they kept on writing-- except my grandmother and quite a few of my mother's cousins who didn't manage to get out and who were then deported and never heard of again.
My, those who got out, they all wrote, make her learn a trade.
Take her out of school.
It's much better if she knows to do something with their hands.
So at the age of 15, I was taken out of school.
And I was put into a milliners-- as a milliner's apprentice, which I did for the next two years.
I hated every minute of it.
In the evening, after every day after school-- after work, my father took me for an hour.
And I had tuition from him.
So I went on learning in a completely different way than we learned in school, but in a much more intelligent way.
And so that I--
Well, it was, I-- I suppose today, they do it anyway already that if they speak about-- when in our school-- our schooling was you had to swat up everything.
You had books.
And you had to swat up those chapters.
I mean, in religion classes, I had to swat up the whole prayer book.
I had to say the whole Shema, the whole [NON-ENGLISH] by heart without understanding practically a word of it.
Whereas-- and the same thing was with history.
And the same thing was with geography.
Nothing had any connection.
When my father taught me, then he took a period and we went through art and-- and everything that happened in that period, the parallel.
We dealt with it.
And that, unfortunately, it stopped after a while.
Because, I mean, we had two years of this-- this tuition.
But I learned, I think, more during those two years than all the previous years, I mean, before all the previous years.
We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-- so I did this for two years.
Then I had a year when I was-- I was teaching children German and-- and also French.
By that time, I spoke German.
I spoke French.
I spoke English and Hungarian.
The French, my-- one of my mother's sisters married a Frenchmen.
And they had no children.
And he was, in a way, my godfather.
And I-- and French-- in Hungary, French was a very important language.
Because and every poet, every writer, every painter spent at least a year in Paris.
And our whole culture was French-orientated.
And therefore, and I was enamored of the language.
I used to sit and listen to-- same like I listen to music.
I listened to French language, so that I-- I learned.
And also, I had the most brilliant French teacher at school, who later on was with me in camp.
And she didn't die, but I-- she died and I visited her then again before she died in our hometown.
But she was the most brilliant teacher that ever existed.
And she got everybody to speak French very, very soon.
Because she-- we adored her to such an extent that nobody would have come to the lesson unprepared.
Now, her name was Magda Szemzo.
And, as I say, we all adored her.
And we-- so I spoke French.
And I spoke-- I learned English from my mother, who taught me quite-- from quite early on.
That's why I still have a German accent and not a Hungarian accent, which normally I would have.
Then I-- so I worked with children.
And that year, that was '41 to '42, 1941 to 1942.
And during that year, I also did a sewing course, a six-week sewing course and a one-week cooking course where I learned to do one cake on a special machine, which was the most useless course.
Anyway, becoming a millinery was also not very useful.
First of all, I always hated the needle.
And secondly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I really didn't-- I found it-- that it wasn't for me.
And I wanted to work with children.
Now, I wanted to go to a training college.
But by that time, we couldn't go to a training college.
A state training college wouldn't accept us.
But in Budapest, the-- the community of [?
Buda ?] instituted a training college for girls who wanted to do nursery nurse and nursery school teachers training.
It was a one-year incredibly intensive and absolutely wonderful training course, quite, quite exciting.
It was really one of my best years in my life, certainly in my youth my best year.
My father didn't want me to go.
Now, I from quite early times, I-- from quite early on, I always earned some money.
I made things.
I made flowers.
I made lamp shades.
I made belts.
I taught children.
But from age of 14, I was earning.
And--
I wanted to have some money.
And I was kept very, very low.
I was kept very low partly from-- because of my parents, my father's principles.
But I also was kept very low, because I didn't have the money.
And my father has-- had-- was very popular.
He had lots and lots of clients.
But most of them didn't pay.
And-- so we had-- and if there was money in the house, then it went for books and music, but not for-- not for-- for other things.
Also, for the tuition, by that time, my brother went up to Budapest to study to the music academy.
And so he needed-- they needed to support him.
And-- and I had very little money.
And so I-- and I wanted money to do things.
And I also was putting aside money for later on.
Because I wanted to go up to Budapest.
I was not happy at home.
My parents' home wasn't a very, very nice home.
They fought a lot.
And they were so very, very-- it was a highly interesting home, but not a very happy home.
And--
That was all the way through my childhood.
And-- I'm-- and-- and-- I mean, many times I thought they should have divorced.
But they didn't divorce, because of us.
And I don't think we were very grateful to them, because of that.
But anyway, so it was a very, very difficult-- it was really pretty difficult living at home.
And I just wanted to get out.
My-- my only wish was to get out of the house, so I was saving.
And when I-- at the age of 18, when I still was minor, I had to have permission.
My father didn't want me to go up to Budapest.
But eventually, he agreed.
But he didn't give me any money except he was willing to pay for my room.
But everything else I had to earn myself.
Now, at this course in Budapest, at this nursery-- nursing-- nursery school teacher's course, you had to have school finishing certificate, I mean, the equivalent of A-levels.
And I didn't have it, because I left school at the age of 15.
But they-- I had an interview.
And then they said that I have to pass a three days intelligence test.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I did pass.
And so they did accept me.
In a way, in certain ways, it was quite difficult.
Because for me, because all the others did have a formal education up this end.
And I was one who did not have a formal education.
But I enjoyed every minute of it.
It was very, very hard.
I was giving lessons again in German and French in my free time.
I also managed to squeeze in singing in two choirs.
And-- and going-- and-- and seeing a little bit things in, I don't know, seeing theater and having friends.
But I only allowed myself four-- four hours to sleep.
Yes.
